channel.title: Library Science on Spyke Space
channel.description: Recent content in Library Science on Spyke Space

item.title: Making Room
item.description:
The first contribution to the new Seed Library wasn’t a seed.

It was a photograph.

A local gardener brought in a picture of their garden with a short note encouraging others to try xeriscaping. We pinned it to a small community board above the seed cabinet. It became the first story in what I hope will become a growing collection.

A few days earlier, I had been removing things.

The checkout log disappeared. We had never really used the information it collected. The brochure became a bookmark. I stopped worrying about maintaining a detailed seed inventory. One by one, I asked whether each part of the Seed Library existed to serve the library or to serve the person standing in front of it.

Some things stayed.

The bookmark stayed because someone might tuck it inside a gardening book and discover it again next spring. A featured gardening book remained because this is still a library. A small cork board stayed because it offered an invitation instead of another instruction.

Everything else quietly fell away.

I had been thinking about stewardship as preserving things.

Preserving the forms.

Preserving the procedures.

Preserving the pieces we’d always had.

But stewardship isn’t only about keeping.

Sometimes it’s about making room.

Making room for curiosity.

Making room for participation.

Making room for people to surprise you.

The first photograph convinced me that people don’t always want another place to record what they’ve taken.

Sometimes they’re looking for a place to leave behind what they’ve learned.

The old Seed Library asked patrons to document a transaction.

The new Seed Library invited them to contribute to a community.

Those sound similar, but they create entirely different experiences.

I don’t know how full that little cork board will become over the years.

I do know that the first thing pinned to it wasn’t a reminder of what left the library.

It was evidence of what came back.

item.title: Outreach Starts Inside
item.description:
I sat down to write a conference proposal with two days left before the deadline.

I wasn’t starting from nothing. In fact, I had almost the opposite problem. I had too much. Two years of projects, experiments, frustrations, maps, partnerships, bookmobile stops, community events, program kits, websites, checklists, conversations, and observations all wanted to be included. Somewhere in all of it was the story I wanted to tell. I just couldn’t see its shape.

So I started talking.

Not about the proposal, but about the work.

I remembered the uncertainty of stepping into a brand-new outreach librarian role while the library itself was still evolving. I remembered believing that my work could only ever be as good as the tools I had to work with. I remembered helping improve collections, signage, spaces, and workflows because I couldn’t imagine inviting someone to discover a library that wasn’t yet ready to tell its own story.

Then another memory surfaced.

There is a particular feeling that has followed me through outreach work. It doesn’t happen when the weather changes or a visit moves to another parking lot. Those moments are expected. This feeling arrives when I find myself asking, What am I actually doing here?

Not because something has gone wrong.

Because I know the library has something meaningful for the people standing in front of me, and yet I don’t have it with me.

That question became the thread that connected everything else.

The grab-and-go program kits. The outdoor reading room. The mobile makerspace ideas. The website. The intake forms. Even the language we use to describe our services. None of those projects existed in isolation. They were all attempts to close the distance between what the library is capable of and what outreach is able to share.

Somewhere in that conversation another sentence appeared, almost by accident.

Outreach is looking inside the library.

It surprised me because it sounded backwards.

We often imagine outreach as the work that happens outside our walls. But before we can carry the library into the community, we have to understand what we’re carrying. Outreach isn’t separate from the library. It’s a reflection of it.

The more I thought about it, the more I realized that I hadn’t been building a bookmobile service. I had been building a mobile expression of the library itself.

Books will always be part of that. But so are storytimes, makerspace activities, reading spaces, conversations, discovery, creativity, and belonging. My outreach work became less about asking, What should I take with me? and more about asking, How can the library show up here?

By the end of the afternoon, the conference proposal had almost written itself.

Not because I finally found the right words.

Because I finally understood what I’d been building all along.

The proposal was simply the first time I said it out loud.

item.title: Tending Subjects
item.description: I’ve been thinking a lot about how to organize Field Notes. At first, I assumed I needed to catalog the topics I wrote about. A note about bubbles should probably be tagged with bubbles. A note about fragrance should probably mention perfume. Before long I found myself imagining endless lists of increasingly specific things to maintain. It felt strangely familiar. Libraries have spent generations thinking about how people discover information. One of the first lessons they teach is that not everything deserves the same kind of stewardship. A search engine is remarkably good at finding words like bubbles, rainstorms, OceanLab, or linen. Those things already live naturally in the writing. Every sentence quietly becomes an index. What deserves more care are the subjects. A bubble experiment might belong to Physical Science. A reflection on organizing this website might belong to Library Science. Those subjects change slowly. They become places where seemingly unrelated Field Notes can discover one another over time. I realized I wasn’t trying to catalog everything. I was tending a landscape. The details are free to grow wild. The landscapes are cared for. There’s something comforting about that. It feels less like organizing a website and more like caring for a garden. Search can wander through the flowers. I only have to tend the paths between them.
